For it is submission (الاستسلام) and humility (الخضوع) verbally by the testimony of monotheism and prophethood, and practically by outward practical following, whether it is accompanied by the belief in the truth of what is testified to and acted upon, or not. By the outward appearance of the two testimonies, blood is protected, and upon it, marriages and inheritances are conducted.

His saying: "And if you obey Allah and His Apostle, He will not stint you anything of [the reward of] your deeds" (49:14). Stinting (الليت) is reduction (النقص). It is said: latahu yalituhu laytan when he reduces it. What is meant by obedience (الإطاعة) is sincerity in it through the agreement of the internal with the external without hypocrisy. Obedience to Allah is responding to what He has called toward in terms of belief and action. Obedience to His Messenger is believing him and following him in what he commands regarding that over which he has authority (الولاية) in the affairs of the nation. What is meant by "deeds" is their reward; what is meant by the reduction of deeds is the reduction of their reward.

The meaning is: If you obey Allah in what He commands you regarding following His religion in belief, and you obey the Messenger in what he commands you, He will not reduce anything from the rewards of your deeds. His saying: "Indeed Allah is All-forgiving, All-merciful" is the reasoning for His not reducing their deeds if they obey Him and His Messenger.

His saying, the Exalted: "The faithful are only those who have attained faith in Allah and His Apostle and then have never doubted, and who wage jihad with their possessions and their persons in the way of Allah. It is they who are the truthful" (49:15). This is a detailed definition of the believers after they were defined generally as those into whose hearts faith has entered, as is necessary from His saying: "You do not have faith" and "faith has not yet entered into your hearts."

His saying: "The faithful are only those who have attained faith in Allah and His Apostle..." contains a restriction (قصر) of the believers to those who have attained faith in Allah and His Messenger, etc. Thus, it serves to define them by the mentioned attributes with a comprehensive and exclusive definition (تعريفاً جامعاً مانعاً). So, whoever is characterized by them is a true believer, just as whoever lacks any of them is not a true believer.

Faith in Allah and His Messenger is the binding of the heart (عقد القلب) to His oneness, the Exalted, the truth of what His Messenger was sent with, the correctness of the message, and following the Messenger in what he commands.

His saying: "and then have never doubted" (49:15). That is, they did not doubt the truth of what they believed in, and their faith was firm and established, not shaken by doubt. The use of "then" (ثم) rather than "and" (الواو) — as has been said — is to indicate the negation of the occurrence of doubt time after time, as if it were always fresh and new. Thus, it implies the establishment of faith upon its initial strength. Had it said "and they did not doubt," it would have been possible for it to be true with faith initially accompanied by the absence of doubt, while remaining silent about what follows.

His saying: "and who wage jihad with their possessions and their persons in the way of Allah" (49:15). Waging jihad (المجاهدة) is the expenditure of effort and energy. The way of Allah (سبيل الله) is His religion. What is meant by jihad with possessions and persons is acting according to what one's ability encompasses and what one's energy reaches in terms of financial obligations, such as zakat and other mandatory expenditures, and physical obligations, such as prayer, fasting, Hajj, and others.

The meaning is: They strive by performing financial and physical obligations while they are, or while their action is, within the religion and way of Allah.

His saying: "It is they who are the truthful" (49:15) is a confirmation of their faith if they possess the mentioned attributes.

His saying, the Exalted: "Say, ‘Will you inform Allah about your religion, while Allah knows whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth, and Allah has knowledge of all things?’" (49:16). This is a rebuke to the Bedouins for saying "We have faith," which implies a claim of truthfulness in their speech and insistence upon it. It is said: When the previous verse was revealed, the Bedouins swore that they were truthful believers in their saying "We have faith," so "Say, ‘Will you inform Allah about your religion...’" was revealed. The meaning of the verse is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: "They count it as a favor to you that they have embraced Islam. Say, ‘Do not count your embracing of Islam as a favor to me. Rather it is Allah who has done you a favor by guiding you to faith, if you are truthful’" (49:17). That is, they count it as a favor to you that they embraced Islam, and they have erred in this counting of favor in two ways: first, that the true blessing in which there is a favor is faith, which is the key to the happiness of this world and the Hereafter, rather than Islam, which has formal benefits such as the protection of blood and the permissibility of marriages and inheritances; second, that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) has nothing of the matter of religion except that he is a Messenger commanded to convey, so there is no favor upon him from anyone who embraces Islam.

If there were any favor, it would be for them upon Allah, the Glorified, because the religion is His religion. However, no one has a favor upon Allah, because the ones who benefit from the religion in this world and the Hereafter are the believers, not Allah, the Absolutely Self-Sufficient. Thus, the favor belongs to Allah over them for having guided them to it.

He subsequently substituted "faith" (الإيمان) for "Islam" (الإسلام) to indicate that the favor is only through faith, not Islam, which only benefits them outwardly.

Thus, His saying: "Say, ‘Do not count your embracing of Islam as a favor to me. Rather it is Allah who has done you a favor...’" etc., included an indication of their error in both respects:

First: Their error in directing the favor toward the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), while he is a Messenger who has no part in the matter. This is indicated by His saying: "Do not count your embracing of Islam as a favor to me."

Second: That the favor — if there were any favor — is only through faith, not Islam. This is indicated by the substitution of faith for Islam.

His saying, the Exalted: "Indeed Allah knows the Unseen of the heavens and the earth, and Allah sees best what you do" (49:18). This is a conclusion to the Surah and a confirmation that reasons and emphasizes all the prohibitions and commands that preceded in the Surah, the realities clarified therein, and what was reported regarding the faith of some and the lack of faith of others. Thus, the verse, by its content, provides the reasoning for all of that.

What is meant by the Unseen (غيب) of the heavens and the earth is what is hidden within them, or more generally, what is in them and what is outside of them.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Muqatil regarding the saying of the Exalted: "O you who have faith! Let not any people ridicule another people" — He said: It was revealed concerning a group from Banu Tamim who mocked Bilal, Salman, Ammar, Khabbab, Suhayb, Ibn Fuhayrah, and Salim, the freed slave of Abu Hudhayfah.

In al-Majma': The saying "Let not any people ridicule another people" was revealed concerning Thabit ibn Qays ibn Shammas. He had a heaviness in his hearing, and when he entered the mosque, people would make room for him until he sat near the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to hear what he said. One day he entered the mosque when the people had finished the prayer and taken their places. He began stepping over the necks of the people, saying: "Make room, make room," until he reached a man. The man said to him: "You have found a place, so sit." So he sat behind him in anger. When the darkness cleared, he said: "Who is this?" The man said: "I am so-and-so." Thabit said: "The son of so-and-so," mentioning a mother of his by whom he used to be shamed in the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية). The man lowered his head in shame, and the verse was revealed. Narrated from Ibn Abbas.

In the same source: And His saying: "nor let women [ridicule] women" was revealed concerning the wives of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) who mocked Umm Salamah. Narrated from Anas. This was because she had tied her waist with a sabibah — which is a white cloth — and let its two ends hang behind her, and she was dragging it. Aisha said to Hafsa: "Look at what she is dragging behind her; it is like the tongue of a dog." This was their ridicule. It is also said that she shamed her for her short stature and pointed with her hand that she was short. Narrated from al-Hasan.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ahmad, Abd ibn Humayd, al-Bukhari in al-Adab, Abu Dawud, al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa'i, Ibn Majah, Abu Ya'la, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, al-Baghawi in his Mu'jam, Ibn Hibban, al-Shirazi in al-Alqab, al-Tabarani, Ibn al-Sunni in Amal al-Yawm wa al-Laylah, and al-Hakim (who authenticated it), Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in Shu'ab al-Iman narrated from Abu Jabirah ibn al-Dahhak, who said: Concerning us, the Banu Salama, "and do not insult one another by [offensive] nicknames" was revealed. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) arrived in Medina, and there was not a man among us except that he had two or three names. When he called one of them by one of those names, they would say: "O Messenger of Allah, he dislikes this name." So Allah revealed: "and do not insult one another by [offensive] nicknames."

In the same source, Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from al-Suddi: Salman al-Farsi was with two men on a journey, serving them and eating from their food. Salman fell into a deep sleep, and his two companions sought him but did not find him. They pitched the tent and said: "Salman wants nothing other than this: to come to prepared food and a pitched tent." When Salman came, they sent him to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to seek some condiment (إدام) for them. He went and came to him, saying: "O Messenger of Allah, my companions have sent me so that you may provide them with condiment if you have any." He said: "What will your companions do with condiment? They have already eaten condiment."

Salman returned and informed them. They set out and came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and said: "By the One who sent you with the truth, we have not touched food since we halted." He said: "You have both eaten Salman as condiment by your speech." Then "Will any of you love to eat the flesh of his dead brother?" was revealed.

In the same source, Diya' al-Maqdisi narrated from Anas, who said: The Arabs used to serve one another during journeys. There was a man with Abu Bakr and Umar who served them. They slept and woke up, and he had not prepared food for them. They said: "This one is indeed a heavy sleeper." They woke him and said: "Go to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and say to him: Abu Bakr and Umar send you greetings of peace and seek condiment from you." He said: "They have already eaten condiment." They came to him and said: "O Messenger of Allah, with what did we eat condiment?" He said: "With the flesh of your brother. By the One in whose hand is my soul, I indeed see his flesh between your teeth." They said: "Seek forgiveness for us, O Messenger of Allah." He said: "Command him to seek forgiveness for you."

I say: It appears that the story mentioned in the two narrations is one and the same. The two men mentioned in the first narration are Abu Bakr and Umar, and the man mentioned in the second is Salman. This is supported by what is in Jawami' al-Jami', which says: It is narrated that Abu Bakr and Umar sent Salman to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to bring them food. He sent him to Usama ibn Zayd, who was the treasurer of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) over his baggage. He said: "I have nothing." He returned to them, and they said: "Usama has been stingy. If we had sent Salman to the well of Samihah, its water would have sunk."

Then they went to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and he said to them: "Why do I see the greenness of flesh in your mouths?" They said: "O Messenger of Allah, we have not partaken of any meat today." He said: "You have done wrong; you are eating the flesh of Salman and Usama." Then it was revealed.

In al-Uyun, with its chain of narration from Muhammad ibn Yahya ibn Abi Abbad from his uncle, who said: I heard al-Rida (peace be upon him) one day reciting poetry — and he rarely recited poetry:

كلنا نأمل مدا في الأجل والمنايا هن آفات الأمل لا يغرنك أباطيل المنى والزم القصد ودع عنك العلل إنما الدنيا كظل زائل حل فيه راكب ثم رحل

"We all hope for an extension in our term, But the fates are the blights of hope. Let not the vanities of wishes deceive you; Adhere to the middle path and leave aside excuses. The world is but a vanishing shadow, In which a rider alights and then departs."

I said: "To whom does this belong, may Allah strengthen the Prince?" He said: "To an Iraqi of yours." I said: "Abu al-Atahiya (1) recited it to me as his own." He said: "Give his name and leave this; indeed Allah, the Glorified, says: 'and do not insult one another by [offensive] nicknames.' Perhaps the man dislikes this."

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from al-Husayn ibn Mukhtar from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: The Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) said in a speech of his: ضع أمر أخيك على أحسنه ـ حتى يأتيك ما يقلبك منه ، ولا تظنن بكلمة خرجت من أخيك سوءا ـ وأنت تجد لها في الخير محملا — "Place the affair of your brother upon its best [interpretation] until there comes to you that which turns you from it. And do not think evil of a word that has come out of your brother while you find for it a possible good meaning."

In Nahj al-Balaghah, he (peace be upon him) said: إذا استولى الصلاح على الزمان وأهله ، ثم أساء رجل الظن برجل لم يظهر منه حوبة فقد ظلم ، وإذا استولى الفساد على الزمان وأهله ـ ثم أحسن رجل الظن برجل فقد غرر — "When righteousness prevails over the time and its people, then if a man thinks ill of a man from whom no sin has appeared, he has done wrong. And when corruption prevails over the time and its people, then if a man thinks well of a man, he has been deceived."

I say: The two narrations are not contradictory. The second looks at the suspicion itself, while the first looks at acting upon its effect in practice.

In al-Khisal, from Asbat ibn Muhammad with his chain of narration to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) that he said: الغيبة أشد من الزنا — "Backbiting is more severe than adultery." It was said: "O Messenger of Allah, why is that?" He said: صاحب الزنا يتوب فيتوب الله عليه ـ وصاحب الغيبة يتوب فلا يتوب الله عليه ـ حتى يكون صاحبه الذي يحله — "The perpetrator of adultery repents and Allah accepts his repentance, but the perpetrator of backbiting repents and Allah does not accept his repentance until his companion [the victim] absolves him."

I say: It was also narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Ibn Mardawayh and al-Bayhaqi from Abu Sa'id and Jabir from him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). Its wording is: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: الغيبة أشد من الزنا — "Backbiting is more severe than adultery." They said: "O Messenger of Allah, how is backbiting more severe than adultery?" He said: إن الرجل يزني فيتوب فيتوب الله عليه ـ وإن صاحب الغيبة لا يغفر له حتى يغفرها له صاحبه — "Indeed, a man commits adultery and repents, and Allah accepts his repentance; but the perpetrator of backbiting is not forgiven until his companion forgives him."

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration to al-Sakuni from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: الغيبة أسرع في دين الرجل المسلم من الأكلة في جوفه — "Backbiting is faster in [destroying] the religion of a Muslim man than gangrene in his interior."

In the same source, with its chain of narration from Hafs ibn Umar from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was asked: "What is the expiation for backbiting?" He said: تستغفر الله لمن اغتبته كما ذكرته — "You seek forgiveness from Allah for the one you backbit, just as you mentioned him."

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding the saying of the Exalted: "and made you nations and tribes" — He said: "The nations (الشعوب) are the non-Arabs and the tribes (القبائل) are the Arabs."

I say: This is also attributed in Majma' al-Bayan to Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh and al-Bayhaqi narrated from Jabir ibn Abdillah, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) addressed us in the middle of the Days of Tashriq during the Farewell Pilgrimage. He said: يا أيها الناس ألا إن ربكم واحد ، ألا إن أباكم واحد ، ألا لا فضل لعربي على عجمي ، ولا لعجمي على عربي ، ولا لأسود على أحمر ولا لأحمر على أسود إلا بالتقوى ـ إن أكرمكم عند الله أتقاكم. ألا هل بلغت؟ — "O mankind! Behold, your Lord is One. Behold, your father is one. Behold, there is no superiority for an Arab over a non-Arab, nor for a non-Arab over an Arab, nor for a black person over a red person, nor for a red person over a black person, except by godwariness. Indeed, the noblest of you in the sight of Allah is the most God-conscious among you. Behold, have I conveyed?" They said: "Yes, O Messenger of Allah." He said: "Then let the witness convey it to the absent."

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Abu Bakr al-Hadrami from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) married Miqdad ibn al-Aswad to Duba'ah bint al-Zubayr ibn Abd al-Muttalib. He only married him to lower [the barriers of] marriages, so that they might follow the example of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and so they might know that the noblest of them in the sight of Allah is the most God-conscious among them.

In Rawdat al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Jamil ibn Darraj, who said: I said to Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): "What is nobility (الكرم)?" He said: "Godwariness (التقوى)."

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Yunus ibn Ya'qub from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) in a tradition, he said: إن الإسلام قبل الإيمان وعليه يتوارثون ـ وعليه يتناكحون والإيمان عليه يثابون — "Indeed, Islam is before faith. Upon it they inherit and upon it they marry, and for faith they are rewarded."

In al-Khisal, from al-A'mash from Ja'far ibn Muhammad (peace be upon him) in a tradition: والإسلام غير الإيمان ، وكل مؤمن مسلم وليس كل مسلم مؤمنا — "Islam is other than faith. Every believer is a Muslim, but not every Muslim is a believer."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, regarding the saying of the Exalted: "The Bedouins say, ‘We have faith’" — Ibn Jarir narrated from Qatadah: Regarding His saying: "The Bedouins say, ‘We have faith’" — He said: It was revealed concerning the Banu Asad.

I say: This is also narrated from Mujahid and others.

In the same source, Ibn Majah, Ibn Mardawayh, al-Tabarani, and al-Bayhaqi in Shu'ab al-Iman narrated from Ali ibn Abi Talib, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: الإيمان معرفة بالقلب وإقرار باللسان وعمل بالأركان — "Faith is recognition with the heart, affirmation with the tongue, and action with the limbs."

In the same source, al-Nasa'i, al-Bazzar, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: The Banu Asad came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and said: "O Messenger of Allah, we have embraced Islam, and the Arabs fought you while we did not fight you." So this verse was revealed: "They count it as a favor to you that they have embraced Islam."

I say: There are other narrations with this meaning.

(1) Al-Atahiya implies a deficiency in intellect.
